lines.text: Imagine each player is dealt a single card ranked from ace down to five, where ace is best.
There is no binary nuts-or-air divide here — every rank from ace to five represents a different level of strength.
At the end of the hand, the player with the higher card wins a showdown.
One player gets to bet or check; the other can call or fold.
Because hands are spread across a full spectrum, the bettor needs to figure out where value ends and bluffing begins.
The strongest hands near the top of the range bet for value because they win at showdown often enough.
The very weakest hands near the bottom bluff because they cannot win at showdown anyway.
The medium hands in between are the interesting ones — they are often better off checking than doing either.
The caller faces an equally interesting problem: call with hands that beat enough of the bettor's value range.
A calling threshold emerges naturally from the pot odds — hands above that line call, hands below fold.
The beauty of this game is that all three zones — value, check-back, and bluff — form from the same ranked range.
You can see that where a hand sits in the continuum determines its role, not just whether it is strong or weak.
